text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Kate
Health Centre

Keele University, Keele, Newcastle

 Staffordshire ST5 5BG
Dear Dr Kate
Daniel Harris, DOB: 14-03-1991, Hospital No: K594203 NHS No. 7719826405
12 Larch Close, Newcastle-under-Lyme, ST5 1AB 

Diagnoses/summary: Idiopathic generalised epilepsy with previous generalised tonic–clonic and myoclonic seizures; patient has generalised epilepsy. Marked clinical improvement since VNS optimisation at the last visit.

MRI brain (2018): No focal structural abnormality reported.
EEG (2023): Generalised spike–wave discharges consistent with generalised epilepsy phenotype.

Previous medication: valproate (effective but weight gain), levetiracetam (behavioural adverse effects), topiramate (cognitive slowing), lamotrigine (partial benefit), clonazepam PRN; trial of zonisamide discontinued due to renal colic.

Current medication: lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily; levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily (low-dose adjunct); folic acid 5 mg daily. Vagus nerve stimulator in situ; output parameters adjusted at last review.

I reviewed Daniel Harris in clinic today. He attended alone and brought a paper seizure diary. Since VNS parameter optimisation at his previous appointment, he feels substantially better in day-to-day functioning and reports improved confidence with community activities. Family and employer feedback has been positive. Sleep routine has become more regular and he has resumed light exercise. He denies new adverse effects aside from occasional throat paraesthesia at stimulation onset, which he finds tolerable. Appetite and weight have stabilised. Mood is described as “more even”, with no self-harm thoughts. He has continued to use the VNS magnet for auras, reporting that it often shortens events.

Seizure control: His family corroborate a clear decline in convulsive activity; there has been only one generalised tonic–clonic event over roughly half a year. Brief myoclonic twitches still occur on some mornings, typically once a month on waking, but these are short and manageable. Importantly, there have been no prolonged seizures, clusters requiring attendance, or status episodes since the VNS changes. Ã‚Â· ï¿½

Examination: Alert and oriented; speech fluent; no focal neurological deficit. VNS pocket site well-healed, no tenderness. Cardiovascular and respiratory exams unremarkable. Weight stable at 74 kg. BP 122/74, pulse 68 regular. â€

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily and levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily.
- Maintain current VNS settings given clinical response; no change required today. Provided education on magnet use and avoidance of common seizure triggers (sleep deprivation, missed doses, excess alcohol). Ã©
- Bloods today: U&E, LFT, FBC to monitor ongoing therapy; thyroid function given patient enquiry about fatigue previously.
- Seizure diary to continue; encouraged ongoing lifestyle regularity and hydration.
- DVLA guidance reiterated; he confirms adherence and understands the requirements.
- Routine follow-up in 6 months, earlier if breakthrough convulsions or adverse effects; remote nurse call in 8 weeks to check stability. 

Overall, there has been a sustained and meaningful improvement in control since VNS optimisation, with better quality of life and no prolonged events. Thank you for your ongoing support in primary care, including prescription continuation and monitoring of bloods.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Brief myoclonic twitches still occur on some mornings, typically once a month on waking

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides current seizure control details. It states: 'there has been only one generalised tonic–clonic event over roughly half a year' and 'Brief myoclonic twitches still occur on some mornings, typically once a month on waking'. According to the rule to select the highest frequency when multiple seizure types are reported, the monthly myoclonic twitches are more frequent than 1 GTC in ~6 months. Thus, the current seizure frequency is 1 per month.
seizure_frequency_number: Brief myoclonic twitches still occur on some mornings, typically once a month on waking
reference: Her parents report a reduction in convulsive events, with only a single tonic-clonic seizure over the past six months. Brief myoclonic jerks persist monthly on awakening but are considered tolerable. There have been no prolonged or status episodes since treatment escalation
